title: Samantha and the Bicycle Nights
author: Dady Fredy
subtitle: A Bedtime Story by Dady Fredy
dedication: For Samantha — my co-pilot, my passenger, my heart.

pages.title: Samantha and the Bicycle Nights
pages.subtitle: A Bedtime Story by Dady Fredy
pages.dedication: For Samantha — my co-pilot, my passenger, my heart.

lines: The sun dipped low past the mango trees,
And the evening hummed with a warm, soft breeze.
Dady Fredy rolled out the bicycle bright
And said, "Hop on, Samantha — let's ride tonight."
She climbed in the back in her little blue seat,
Her sandals dangling above the street.
She wrapped her arms round Dady's waist
And off they went — no need to haste.
The pedals turned and the tires sang low,
Past tricycles humming and carinderias' glow.
The streetlamps blinked on, one by one,
As Angeles City cooled down from the sun.
They rode through the gate to the Parade Grounds wide,
Where the old Clark runway stretched far on each side.
The grass smelled sweet and the air was still,
And the sky turned purple past the far-off hill.
"Look, Dady! Airplane!" Samantha would shout,
As the big lights blinked and the engines roared out.
They watched it climb through the tangerine sky
And waved it goodbye as it rumbled on by.
Another one came with its landing lights on,
So low you could almost reach up and hold on.
It thundered above them — then quiet again.
Just crickets, and Dady, and Samantha. Amen.
They pedaled to Starbucks — all glowy and warm,
Where Dady got coffee, his usual form.
And Samantha got… "Ice cream, please! The pink one!"
And the barista smiled: "Coming right up, little sun."
Then on to 7-Eleven — the bright, humming store —
For donuts in boxes (they picked out three… four).
"This chocolate one's Mammy's," Samantha declared,
"And this sprinkle one too — because Mammy has shared."
The box sat behind them, tied tight to the rack,
And Samantha leaned warm against Dady's broad back.
The streets had grown quiet, the city half-dreaming,
The moon had come out — soft and silver and gleaming.
Her eyes started closing, her arms going slack —
So Dady pulled over beside the dark track.
He took off his belt and he looped it around
Both Dady and Samantha — together, safe, bound.
"There," Dady whispered, "now lean on me, love.
The moon's right beside us, the stars up above.
I'll pedal us home through the quiet night air —
You sleep, little anak. Dady Fredy is here."
The bicycle hummed down the last empty lane,
Past sleeping sari-saris and one stray that came
To walk alongside them a block, maybe two —
As if even the world wanted to take care of you.
And Mammy was waiting with the porch light still on.
She opened the door and she whispered, "My dawn."
She took the warm donuts, she kissed Samantha's head,
And together they carried her softly to bed.
So close your bright eyes now, my Samantha, my dear,
The bicycle's resting, the donuts are here.
The airplanes are sleeping on runways of dreams —
And Dady Fredy loves you more than it seems.
Goodnight, Samantha.
Goodnight, anak.
